question:
For every $n$ in the set $\mathrm{N} = \{1,2,\dots \}$ of positive integers,
let $r_n$ be the minimum value of $|c-d\sqrt{3}|$ for all nonnegative
integers $c$ and $d$ with $c+d=n$. Find, with proof, the smallest
positive real number $g$ with $r_n \leq g$ for all $n \in \mathrm{N}$.
solution:
Solution. Let \( g=(1+\sqrt{3}) / 2 \). For each fixed \( n \), the sequence \( n,(n-1)-\sqrt{3} \), \( (n-2)-2 \sqrt{3}, \ldots,-n \sqrt{3} \) is an arithmetic sequence with common difference \( -2 g \) and with terms on both sides of 0 , so there exists a unique term \( x_{n} \) in it with \( -g \leq x_{n}<g \). Then \( r_{n}=\left|x_{n}\right| \leq g \).

For \( x \in \mathbb{R} \), let " \( x \bmod 1 \) " denote \( x-\lfloor x\rfloor \in[0,1) \). Since \( \sqrt{3} \) is irrational, \( \left\{(-d \sqrt{3}) \bmod 1: d \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}\right\} \)is dense in \( [0,1) \). (See the remark below.) Hence for any \( \epsilon>0 \), we can find a positive integer \( d \) such that \( ((-d \sqrt{3}) \bmod 1) \in(g-1-\epsilon, g-1) \). Then \( c-d \sqrt{3} \in(g-\epsilon, g) \) for some integer \( c \geq 0 \). Let \( n=c+d \). Then \( r_{n}=x_{n}=c-d \sqrt{3}>g-\epsilon \) by the uniqueness of \( x_{n} \) above. Thus \( g \) cannot be lowered.

Remark. Let \( \alpha \) be irrational, and for \( n \geq 1 \), let \( a_{n}=(n \alpha \bmod 1) \in[0,1) \). Let us explain why \( \left\{a_{n}: n=1,2, \ldots\right\} \) is dense in \( [0,1] \) when \( \alpha \) is irrational. Given a large integer \( N>0 \), the Pigeonhole Principle [Lar1, Ch. 2.6] produces two integers \( p, q \in\{1,2, \ldots, N+1\} \) such that \( a_{p} \) and \( a_{q} \) fall into the same subinterval \( [i / N,(i+1) / N) \) for some \( 0 \leq i \leq N-1 \). Assume \( q>p \). Then \( (q-p) \alpha \) is congruent modulo 1 to a real number \( r \) with \( |r|<1 / N \). Since \( \alpha \) is irrational, \( r \neq 0 \). The multiples of \( (q-p) \alpha \), taken modulo 1 , will then pass within \( 1 / N \) of any number in \( [0,1] \). This argument applies for any \( N \), so any nonempty open subset of \( [0,1] \) will contain some \( a_{n} \).

In fact, one can prove more, namely that the sequence \( a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots \) is equidistributed in \( [0,1] \) : this means that for each subinterval \( [a, b] \subseteq[0,1] \),
\[
\lim _{M \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\#\left\{n: 1 \leq n \leq M \text { and } a_{n} \in[a, b]\right\}}{M}=b-a .
\]

One way to show this is to observe that the range \( \{1, \ldots, M\} \) can, up to an error of \( o(M) \) terms if \( M \) is much larger than \( N(q-p) \), be partitioned into \( N \)-term arithmetic sequences of the shape \( c, c+(q-p), \ldots, c+(N-1)(q-p) \) (notation as in the previous paragraph), and the \( a_{n} \) for \( n \) in this sequence will be approximately evenly spaced over \( [0,1] \) when \( N \) is large.

Equidistribution can also be deduced from Weyl's Equidistribution Theorem [Kor, Theorem 3.1'], which states that a sequence \( a_{1}, a_{2}, \ldots \) of real numbers is equidistributed modulo 1 if and only if
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{h-1}^{n} \chi_{m}\left(a_{k}\right)=0
\]
for all nonzero integers \( m \), where \( \chi_{m}(x)=e^{2 \pi i m x} \). In our application, if we set \( \omega=e^{2 \pi i \sqrt{3}} \), then the limit in (1) is
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \omega^{k m}=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n}\left(\frac{1-\omega^{(n+1) m}}{1-\omega^{m}}\right)=0
\]
since \( \left|1-\omega^{(n+1) m}\right| \leq 2 \), while \( 1-\omega^{m} \neq 0 \), since \( \sqrt{3} \) is irrational. (See Solution 4 to 1990A2 for another application of the density result, and see Solution 2 to 1995B6 for an application of the equidistribution of \( (n \alpha \bmod 1) \) for irrational \( \alpha \). See 1995B6 also for a multidimensional generalization of the equidistribution result.)

descriptive_long.question:
For every $indexvalue$ in the set $\mathrm{upperlimit} = \{1,2,\dots \}$ of positive integers,
let $minoffset$ be the minimum value of $|countvar-diffvar\sqrt{3}|$ for all nonnegative
integers $countvar$ and $diffvar$ with $countvar+diffvar=indexvalue$. Find, with proof, the smallest
positive real number $boundgap$ with $minoffset \leq boundgap$ for all $indexvalue \in \mathrm{upperlimit}$.
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let \( boundgap=(1+\sqrt{3}) / 2 \). For each fixed \( indexvalue \), the sequence \( indexvalue,(indexvalue-1)-\sqrt{3} \), \( (indexvalue-2)-2 \sqrt{3}, \ldots,-indexvalue \sqrt{3} \) is an arithmetic sequence with common difference \( -2 boundgap \) and with terms on both sides of 0 , so there exists a unique term \( sequencex \) in it with \( -boundgap \leq sequencex<boundgap \). Then \( minoffset=\left|sequencex\right| \leq boundgap \).

For \( realvalue \in \mathbb{R} \), let " \( realvalue \bmod 1 \) " denote \( realvalue-\lfloor realvalue\rfloor \in[0,1) \). Since \( \sqrt{3} \) is irrational, \( \left\{(-diffvar \sqrt{3}) \bmod 1: diffvar \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}\right\} \)is dense in \( [0,1) \). (See the remark below.) Hence for any \( smallerror>0 \), we can find a positive integer \( diffvar \) such that \( ((-diffvar \sqrt{3}) \bmod 1) \in(boundgap-1-smallerror, boundgap-1) \). Then \( countvar-diffvar \sqrt{3} \in(boundgap-smallerror, boundgap) \) for some integer \( countvar \geq 0 \). Let \( indexvalue=countvar+diffvar \). Then \( minoffset=sequencex=countvar-diffvar \sqrt{3}>boundgap-smallerror \) by the uniqueness of \( sequencex \) above. Thus \( boundgap \) cannot be lowered.

Remark. Let \( parameteralpha \) be irrational, and for \( indexvalue \geq 1 \), let \( seqvalue=(indexvalue parameteralpha \bmod 1) \in[0,1) \). Let us explain why \( \left\{seqvalue: indexvalue=1,2, \ldots\right\} \) is dense in \( [0,1] \) when \( parameteralpha \) is irrational. Given a large integer \( upperlimit>0 \), the Pigeonhole Principle [Lar1, Ch. 2.6] produces two integers \( firstpick, secondpick \in\{1,2, \ldots, upperlimit+1\} \) such that \( seqatfirst \) and \( seqatsecond \) fall into the same subinterval \( [i / upperlimit,(i+1) / upperlimit) \) for some \( 0 \leq i \leq upperlimit-1 \). Assume \( secondpick>firstpick \). Then \( (secondpick-firstpick) parameteralpha \) is congruent modulo 1 to a real number \( smallreal \) with \( |smallreal|<1 / upperlimit \). Since \( parameteralpha \) is irrational, \( smallreal \neq 0 \). The multiples of \( (secondpick-firstpick) parameteralpha \), taken modulo 1 , will then pass within \( 1 / upperlimit \) of any number in \( [0,1] \). This argument applies for any \( upperlimit \), so any nonempty open subset of \( [0,1] \) will contain some \( seqvalue \).

In fact, one can prove more, namely that the sequence \( boundleft_{1}, boundleft_{2}, \ldots \) is equidistributed in \( [0,1] \) : this means that for each subinterval \( [boundleft, boundright] \subseteq[0,1] \),
\[
\lim _{samplemax \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\#\left\{indexvalue: 1 \leq indexvalue \leq samplemax \text { and } seqvalue \in[boundleft, boundright]\right\}}{samplemax}=boundright-boundleft .
\]

One way to show this is to observe that the range \( \{1, \ldots, samplemax\} \) can, up to an error of \( o(samplemax) \) terms if \( samplemax \) is much larger than \( upperlimit(secondpick-firstpick) \), be partitioned into \( upperlimit \)-term arithmetic sequences of the shape \( countvar, countvar+(secondpick-firstpick), \ldots, countvar+(upperlimit-1)(secondpick-firstpick) \) (notation as in the previous paragraph), and the \( seqvalue \) for \( indexvalue \) in this sequence will be approximately evenly spaced over \( [0,1] \) when \( upperlimit \) is large.

Equidistribution can also be deduced from Weyl's Equidistribution Theorem [Kor, Theorem 3.1'], which states that a sequence \( boundleft_{1}, boundleft_{2}, \ldots \) of real numbers is equidistributed modulo 1 if and only if
\[
\lim _{indexvalue \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{indexvalue} \sum_{helperidx-1}^{indexvalue} characterchi\left(boundleft_{loopindex}\right)=0
\]
for all nonzero integers \( indexmode \), where \( characterchi(realvalue)=e^{2 \pi i indexmode realvalue} \). In our application, if we set \( complexomega=e^{2 \pi i \sqrt{3}} \), then the limit in (1) is
\[
\lim _{indexvalue \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{indexvalue} \sum_{loopindex=1}^{indexvalue} complexomega^{loopindex indexmode}=\lim _{indexvalue \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{indexvalue}\left(\frac{1-complexomega^{(indexvalue+1) indexmode}}{1-complexomega^{indexmode}}\right)=0
\]
since \( \left|1-complexomega^{(indexvalue+1) indexmode}\right| \leq 2 \), while \( 1-complexomega^{indexmode} \neq 0 \), since \( \sqrt{3} \) is irrational. (See Solution 4 to 1990A2 for another application of the density result, and see Solution 2 to 1995B6 for an application of the equidistribution of \( (indexvalue parameteralpha \bmod 1) \) for irrational \( parameteralpha \). See 1995B6 also for a multidimensional generalization of the equidistribution result.)

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
For every $tangerine$ in the set $\mathrm{watermelon} = \{1,2,\dots \}$ of positive integers,
let $cantaloupe$ be the minimum value of $|blueberry-strawberry\sqrt{3}|$ for all nonnegative
integers $blueberry$ and $strawberry$ with $blueberry+strawberry=tangerine$. Find, with proof, the smallest
positive real number $dragonfruit$ with $cantaloupe \leq dragonfruit$ for all $tangerine \in \mathrm{watermelon}$.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let \( dragonfruit=(1+\sqrt{3})/2 \). For each fixed \( tangerine \), the sequence \( tangerine,(tangerine-1)-\sqrt{3},(tangerine-2)-2\sqrt{3},\ldots,-tangerine\sqrt{3} \) is an arithmetic sequence with common difference \( -2\,dragonfruit \) and with terms on both sides of 0, so there exists a unique term \( blackberry \) in it with \( -dragonfruit \leq blackberry < dragonfruit \). Then \( cantaloupe=\lvert blackberry\rvert \leq dragonfruit \).

For \( pineapple \in \mathbb{R} \), let ``\( pineapple \bmod 1 \)'' denote \( pineapple-\lfloor pineapple\rfloor \in [0,1) \). Since \( \sqrt{3} \) is irrational, \( \{(-strawberry\sqrt{3}) \bmod 1: strawberry \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}\} \) is dense in \( [0,1) \). Hence for any \( acerolajam>0 \) we can find a positive integer \( strawberry \) such that \( ((-strawberry\sqrt{3}) \bmod 1) \in (dragonfruit-1-acerolajam,\,dragonfruit-1) \). Then \( blueberry-strawberry\sqrt{3} \in (dragonfruit-acerolajam,\,dragonfruit) \) for some integer \( blueberry \ge 0 \). Let \( tangerine=blueberry+strawberry \). Then \( cantaloupe=blackberry=blueberry-strawberry\sqrt{3} > dragonfruit-acerolajam \) by the uniqueness of \( blackberry \) above. Thus \( dragonfruit \) cannot be lowered.

Remark. Let \( jackfruits \) be irrational, and for \( tangerine \ge 1 \) let \( boysenberry=(tangerine\,jackfruits \bmod 1) \in [0,1) \). We explain why \( \{boysenberry: tangerine=1,2,\ldots\} \) is dense in \( [0,1] \) when \( jackfruits \) is irrational. Given a large integer \( watermelon>0 \), the Pigeonhole Principle produces two integers \( raspberry,cranberry \in \{1,2,\ldots,watermelon+1\} \) such that \( elderberry \) and \( mulberries \) fall into the same subinterval \( [i/watermelon,(i+1)/watermelon) \) for some \( 0 \le i \le watermelon-1 \). Assume \( cranberry>raspberry \). Then \( (cranberry-raspberry)\,jackfruits \) is congruent modulo 1 to a real number \( plantain \) with \( |plantain|<1/watermelon \). Since \( jackfruits \) is irrational, \( plantain \neq 0 \). The multiples of \( (cranberry-raspberry)\,jackfruits \), taken modulo 1, therefore pass within \( 1/watermelon \) of any number in \( [0,1] \). This argument applies for any \( watermelon \); hence any nonempty open subset of \( [0,1] \) contains some \( boysenberry \).

In fact, one can prove more, namely that the sequence \( boysenberry \) is equidistributed in \( [0,1] \): this means that for each subinterval \( [honeydew,nectarine] \subseteq [0,1] \),
\[
\lim_{persimmon\to\infty}\frac{\#\{tangerine:1\le tangerine\le persimmon\text{ and } boysenberry\in[honeydew,nectarine]\}}{persimmon}=nectarine-honeydew.
\]

One way to show this is to observe that the range \( \{1,\ldots,persimmon\} \) can, up to an error of \( o(persimmon) \) terms if \( persimmon \) is much larger than \( watermelon(cranberry-raspberry) \), be partitioned into \( watermelon \)-term arithmetic sequences of the form \( blueberry,\,blueberry+(cranberry-raspberry),\ldots,\,blueberry+(watermelon-1)(cranberry-raspberry) \); the \( boysenberry \) for \( tangerine \) in such a sequence will be approximately evenly spaced over \( [0,1] \) when \( watermelon \) is large.

Equidistribution can also be deduced from Weyl's Equidistribution Theorem, which states that a sequence \( a_{1},a_{2},\ldots \) of real numbers is equidistributed modulo 1 if and only if
\[
\lim_{tangerine\to\infty}\frac{1}{tangerine}\sum_{grapefruit-1}^{tangerine} cloudberry\!\left(a_{passionfr}\right)=0
\]
for all non-zero integers \( kumquats \), where \( cloudberry(x)=e^{2\pi i\,kumquats x} \). In our application, if we set \( lycheecake=e^{2\pi i\sqrt{3}} \), then the limit above is
\[
\lim_{tangerine\to\infty}\frac{1}{tangerine}\sum_{passionfr=1}^{tangerine} lycheecake^{passionfr\,kumquats}
 =\lim_{tangerine\to\infty}\frac{1}{tangerine}\left(\frac{1-lycheecake^{(tangerine+1)\,kumquats}}{1-lycheecake^{\,kumquats}}\right)=0,
\]
since \( |1-lycheecake^{(tangerine+1)\,kumquats}|\le 2 \) while \( 1-lycheecake^{\,kumquats}\neq 0 \) because \( \sqrt{3} \) is irrational.

(See Solution 4 to 1990A2 for another application of the density result, and see Solution 2 to 1995B6 for an application of the equidistribution of \( (tangerine\,jackfruits \bmod 1) \) for irrational \( jackfruits \). See 1995B6 also for a multidimensional generalization of the equidistribution result.)

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
For every $scarcityindex$ in the set $\mathrm{limitationmeasure} = \{1,2,\dots \}$ of positive integers,
let $proximitybatch$ be the minimum value of $|deficittotal-deprivation\sqrt{3}|$ for all nonnegative
integers $deficittotal$ and $deprivation$ with $deficittotal+deprivation=scarcityindex$. Find, with proof, the smallest
positive real number $magnitudepeak$ with $proximitybatch \leq magnitudepeak$ for all $scarcityindex \in \mathrm{limitationmeasure}$.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let \( magnitudepeak=(1+\sqrt{3}) / 2 \). For each fixed \( scarcityindex \), the sequence \( scarcityindex,(scarcityindex-1)-\sqrt{3} \), \( (scarcityindex-2)-2 \sqrt{3}, \ldots,-scarcityindex \sqrt{3} \) is an arithmetic sequence with common difference \( -2 magnitudepeak \) and with terms on both sides of 0, so there exists a unique term \( knownstatebatch \) in it with \( -magnitudepeak \leq knownstatebatch<magnitudepeak \). Then \( proximitybatch=\left|knownstatebatch\right| \leq magnitudepeak \).

For \( knownstate \in \mathbb{R} \), let "\( knownstate \bmod 1 \)" denote \( knownstate-\lfloor knownstate\rfloor \in[0,1) \). Since \( \sqrt{3} \) is irrational, \( \left\{(-deprivation \sqrt{3}) \bmod 1: deprivation \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}\right\} \) is dense in \( [0,1) \). (See the remark below.) Hence for any \( \gigantism>0 \), we can find a positive integer \( deprivation \) such that \( ((-deprivation \sqrt{3}) \bmod 1) \in(magnitudepeak-1-\gigantism, magnitudepeak-1) \). Then \( deficittotal-deprivation \sqrt{3} \in(magnitudepeak-\gigantism, magnitudepeak) \) for some integer \( deficittotal \geq 0 \). Let \( scarcityindex=deficittotal+deprivation \). Then \( proximitybatch=knownstatebatch=deficittotal-deprivation \sqrt{3}>magnitudepeak-\gigantism \) by the uniqueness of \( knownstatebatch \) above. Thus \( magnitudepeak \) cannot be lowered.

Remark. Let \( \antirational \) be irrational, and for \( scarcityindex \geq 1 \), let \( endmarkerbatch=(scarcityindex \antirational \bmod 1) \in[0,1) \). Let us explain why \( \left\{endmarkerbatch: scarcityindex=1,2, \ldots\right\} \) is dense in \( [0,1] \) when \( \antirational \) is irrational. Given a large integer \( limitationmeasure>0 \), the Pigeonhole Principle [Lar1, Ch. 2.6] produces two integers \( nextvalue, prevvalue \in\{1,2, \ldots, limitationmeasure+1\} \) such that \( endmarkerprev \) and \( endmarkernext \) fall into the same subinterval \( [i / limitationmeasure,(i+1) / limitationmeasure) \) for some \( 0 \leq i \leq limitationmeasure-1 \). Assume \( prevvalue>nextvalue \). Then \( (prevvalue-nextvalue) \antirational \) is congruent modulo 1 to a real number \( proximity \) with \( |proximity|<1 / limitationmeasure \). Since \( \antirational \) is irrational, \( proximity \neq 0 \). The multiples of \( (prevvalue-nextvalue) \antirational \), taken modulo 1 , will then pass within \( 1 / limitationmeasure \) of any number in \( [0,1] \). This argument applies for any \( limitationmeasure \), so any nonempty open subset of \( [0,1] \) will contain some \( endmarkerbatch \).

In fact, one can prove more, namely that the sequence \( endmarker_{1}, endmarker_{2}, \ldots \) is equidistributed in \( [0,1] \) : this means that for each subinterval \( [endmarker, startmark] \subseteq[0,1] \),
\[
\lim _{miniquantity \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\#\left\{scarcityindex: 1 \leq scarcityindex \leq miniquantity \text { and } endmarkerbatch \in[endmarker, startmark]\right\}}{miniquantity}=startmark-endmarker .
\]

One way to show this is to observe that the range \{1, \ldots, miniquantity\} can, up to an error of \( o(miniquantity) \) terms if \( miniquantity \) is much larger than \( limitationmeasure(prevvalue-nextvalue) \), be partitioned into \( limitationmeasure \)-term arithmetic sequences of the shape \( deficittotal, deficittotal+(prevvalue-nextvalue), \ldots, deficittotal+(limitationmeasure-1)(prevvalue-nextvalue) \) (notation as in the previous paragraph), and the \( endmarkerbatch \) for \( scarcityindex \) in this sequence will be approximately evenly spaced over \( [0,1] \) when \( limitationmeasure \) is large.

Equidistribution can also be deduced from Weyl's Equidistribution Theorem [Kor, Theorem 3.1'], which states that a sequence \( endmarker_{1}, endmarker_{2}, \ldots \) of real numbers is equidistributed modulo 1 if and only if
\[
\lim _{scarcityindex \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{scarcityindex} \sum_{motionless-1}^{scarcityindex} \indifference_{macrovalue}\left(endmarker_{steadystate}\right)=0
\]
for all nonzero integers \( macrovalue \), where \( \indifference_{macrovalue}(knownstate)=e^{2 \pi i macrovalue knownstate} \). In our application, if we set \( \triviality=e^{2 \pi i \sqrt{3}} \), then the limit in (1) is
\[
\lim _{scarcityindex \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{scarcityindex} \sum_{steadystate=1}^{scarcityindex} \triviality^{steadystate macrovalue}=\lim _{scarcityindex \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{scarcityindex}\left(\frac{1-\triviality^{(scarcityindex+1) macrovalue}}{1-\triviality^{macrovalue}}\right)=0
\]
since \( \left|1-\triviality^{(scarcityindex+1) macrovalue}\right| \leq 2 \), while \( 1-\triviality^{macrovalue} \neq 0 \), since \( \sqrt{3} \) is irrational.

garbled_string.question:
For every $qzxwvtnp$ in the set $\mathrm{hjgrksla} = \{1,2,\dots \}$ of positive integers,
let $xcmnqftetq$ be the minimum value of $|pmdvlfqe-sxjzkrth\sqrt{3}|$ for all nonnegative
integers $pmdvlfqe$ and $sxjzkrth$ with $pmdvlfqe+sxjzkrth=qzxwvtnp$. Find, with proof, the smallest
positive real number $dqsmplgh$ with $xcmnqftetq \leq dqsmplgh$ for all $qzxwvtnp \in \mathrm{hjgrksla}$.
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let \( dqsmplgh=(1+\sqrt{3}) / 2 \). For each fixed \( qzxwvtnp \), the sequence \( qzxwvtnp,(qzxwvtnp-1)-\sqrt{3} \), \( (qzxwvtnp-2)-2 \sqrt{3}, \ldots,-qzxwvtnp \sqrt{3} \) is an arithmetic sequence with common difference \( -2 dqsmplgh \) and with terms on both sides of 0 , so there exists a unique term \( gnvlwrydtq \) in it with \( -dqsmplgh \leq gnvlwrydtq<dqsmplgh \). Then \( xcmnqftetq=\left|gnvlwrydtq\right| \leq dqsmplgh \).

For \( gnvlwryd \in \mathbb{R} \), let " \( gnvlwryd \bmod 1 \) " denote \( gnvlwryd-\lfloor gnvlwryd\rfloor \in[0,1) \). Since \( \sqrt{3} \) is irrational, \( \left\{(-sxjzkrth \sqrt{3}) \bmod 1: sxjzkrth \in \mathbb{Z}^{+}\right\} \) is dense in \( [0,1) \). (See the remark below.) Hence for any \( qldhrxmv>0 \), we can find a positive integer \( sxjzkrth \) such that \( ((-sxjzkrth \sqrt{3}) \bmod 1) \in(dqsmplgh-1-qldhrxmv, dqsmplgh-1) \). Then \( pmdvlfqe-sxjzkrth \sqrt{3} \in(dqsmplgh-qldhrxmv, dqsmplgh) \) for some integer \( pmdvlfqe \geq 0 \). Let \( qzxwvtnp=pmdvlfqe+sxjzkrth \). Then \( xcmnqftetq=gnvlwrydtq=pmdvlfqe-sxjzkrth \sqrt{3}>dqsmplgh-qldhrxmv \) by the uniqueness of \( gnvlwrydtq \) above. Thus \( dqsmplgh \) cannot be lowered.

Remark. Let \( jczvwmnp \) be irrational, and for \( qzxwvtnp \geq 1 \), let \( fsdzplmvtq=(qzxwvtnp jczvwmnp \bmod 1) \in[0,1) \). Let us explain why \( \left\{fsdzplmvtq: qzxwvtnp=1,2, \ldots\right\} \) is dense in \( [0,1] \) when \( jczvwmnp \) is irrational. Given a large integer \( hjgrksla>0 \), the Pigeonhole Principle [Lar1, Ch. 2.6] produces two integers \( tblskqhd, vcrhwjys \in\{1,2, \ldots, hjgrksla+1\} \) such that \( fsdzplmvtb \) and \( fsdzplmvtv \) fall into the same subinterval \( [i / hjgrksla,(i+1) / hjgrksla) \) for some \( 0 \leq i \leq hjgrksla-1 \). Assume \( vcrhwjys>tblskqhd \). Then \( (vcrhwjys-tblskqhd) jczvwmnp \) is congruent modulo 1 to a real number \( xcmnqfte \) with \( |xcmnqfte|<1 / hjgrksla \). Since \( jczvwmnp \) is irrational, \( xcmnqfte \neq 0 \). The multiples of \( (vcrhwjys-tblskqhd) jczvwmnp \), taken modulo 1 , will then pass within \( 1 / hjgrksla \) of any number in \( [0,1] \). This argument applies for any \( hjgrksla \), so any nonempty open subset of \( [0,1] \) will contain some \( fsdzplmvtq \).

In fact, one can prove more, namely that the sequence \( fsdzplmv_{1}, fsdzplmv_{2}, \ldots \) is equidistributed in \( [0,1] \) : this means that for each subinterval \( [fsdzplmv, lqwgdkhn] \subseteq[0,1] \),
\[
\lim _{bnvctwle \rightarrow \infty} \frac{\#\left\{qzxwvtnp: 1 \leq qzxwvtnp \leq bnvctwle \text { and } fsdzplmvtq \in[fsdzplmv, lqwgdkhn]\right\}}{bnvctwle}=lqwgdkhn-fsdzplmv .
\]

One way to show this is to observe that the range \( \{1, \ldots, bnvctwle\} \) can, up to an error of \( o(bnvctwle) \) terms if \( bnvctwle \) is much larger than \( hjgrksla(vcrhwjys-tblskqhd) \), be partitioned into \( hjgrksla \)-term arithmetic sequences of the shape \( pmdvlfqe, pmdvlfqe+(vcrhwjys-tblskqhd), \ldots, pmdvlfqe+(hjgrksla-1)(vcrhwjys-tblskqhd) \) (notation as in the previous paragraph), and the \( fsdzplmvtq \) for \( qzxwvtnp \) in this sequence will be approximately evenly spaced over \( [0,1] \) when \( hjgrksla \) is large.

Equidistribution can also be deduced from Weyl's Equidistribution Theorem [Kor, Theorem 3.1'], which states that a sequence \( fsdzplmv_{1}, fsdzplmv_{2}, \ldots \) of real numbers is equidistributed modulo 1 if and only if
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} \sum_{kfdjlsqm-1}^{qzxwvtnp} ufywnkqz\left(fsdzplmvtq\right)=0
\]
for all nonzero integers \( wdkfjqps \), where \( ufywnkqz(gnvlwryd)=e^{2 \pi i wdkfjqps gnvlwryd} \). In our application, if we set \( ptzkmvhs=e^{2 \pi i \sqrt{3}} \), then the limit in (1) is
\[
\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp} \sum_{zmxnghrt=1}^{qzxwvtnp} ptzkmvhs^{zmxnghrt wdkfjqps}=\lim _{qzxwvtnp \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\left(\frac{1-ptzkmvhs^{(qzxwvtnp+1) wdkfjqps}}{1-ptzkmvhs^{wdkfjqps}}\right)=0
\]
since \( \left|1-ptzkmvhs^{(qzxwvtnp+1) wdkfjqps}\right| \leq 2 \), while \( 1-ptzkmvhs^{wdkfjqps} \neq 0 \), since \( \sqrt{3} \) is irrational. (See Solution 4 to 1990A2 for another application of the density result, and see Solution 2 to 1995B6 for an application of the equidistribution of \( (qzxwvtnp jczvwmnp \bmod 1) \) for irrational \( jczvwmnp \). See 1995B6 also for a multidimensional generalization of the equidistribution result.)

kernel_variant.question:
Let $m\ge 2$ be a fixed integer and put  
\[
s_{n}(m)\;=\;
\min\Bigl\{\lvert a-b\sqrt{3}\rvert :\;
a,b\in\mathbb Z_{\ge 0},\; a+mb=n\Bigr\},
\qquad 
g_{m}\;=\;\sup_{n\ge 1}s_{n}(m).
\]

(a)  Prove that  
\[
\boxed{\; g_{m}\;=\;\max\!\Bigl\{\,m-1,\;\dfrac{m+\sqrt{3}}{2}\Bigr\} \;}
\tag{$\star$}
\]

(b)  Show that the set $\{s_{n}(m):\,n\ge 1\}$ is dense in the closed
interval $[0,g_{m}]$ if and only if $m\in\{2,3\}$.

(c)  Assume $m\in\{2,3\}$.  Prove that the sequence
$\bigl(s_{n}(m)\bigr)_{n\ge 1}$ is equidistributed on $[0,g_{m}]$; that
is, for every $0\le\alpha<\beta\le g_{m}$ one has  
\[
\lim_{N\to\infty}\;
\frac{1}{N}\,
\#\Bigl\{1\le n\le N:\;
  s_{n}(m)\in(\alpha,\beta)\Bigr\}
\;=\;
\frac{\beta-\alpha}{g_{m}}.
\]

(d)  Deduce that for $m\in\{2,3\}$ and every $\varepsilon>0$ the
inequality $s_{n}(m)>g_{m}-\varepsilon$ is satisfied for infinitely many
$n$, and that this assertion fails for every $m\ge 4$.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout we abbreviate  
\[
\lambda:=m+\sqrt{3},\qquad
\gamma:=\frac{\lambda}{2}=\frac{m+\sqrt{3}}{2},\qquad
G:=m-1 .
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
1.\;Determination of $g_{m}$ - proof of $(\star)$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

1.1\;A trivial lower bound.  
Choosing $b=0$ and $n=m-1$ gives
$s_{m-1}(m)=m-1=G$, hence $g_{m}\ge G$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
1.2\;A $\gamma$-bound valid for every $n\ge m$.
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Lemma 1.  
For every integer $n\ge m$ there exists an admissible
$b\le n/m$ such that
\[
\lvert n-\lambda b\rvert\le\gamma .
\tag{1.1}
\]

Proof.  
Put $B:=\lfloor n/m\rfloor\;( \ge 1)$ and consider the arithmetic
progression
\[
y_{k}:=n-\lambda k\qquad(0\le k\le B).
\]
One has $y_{0}=n>0$, while
\[
y_{B}\;\le\;n-\lambda\,\frac{n}{m}
      \;=\;n\Bigl(1-\frac{\lambda}{m}\Bigr)
      \;=\;-\frac{n\sqrt{3}}{m}\;<0 .
\]
Consequently there is an index $k_{0}$ with
$y_{k_{0}}\ge 0>y_{k_{0}+1}$.  Since
$y_{k_{0}+1}=y_{k_{0}}-\lambda$, at least one of
$y_{k_{0}},y_{k_{0}+1}$ has absolute value at most
$\lambda/2=\gamma$.  The corresponding $b\in\{k_{0},k_{0}+1\}$ satisfies
$b\le B\le n/m$, so it is admissible. \hfill$\square$

Consequences.  
For every $n\ge m$ we therefore have $s_{n}(m)\le\gamma$.  Combining
this with $s_{n}(m)=n$ when $n<m$ yields the uniform estimate
\[
s_{n}(m)\le
\begin{cases}
n,& n<m,\\[3pt]
\gamma,& n\ge m.
\end{cases}
\tag{1.2}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
1.3\;The case $m\ge 4$.
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Because $\gamma<G$ for $m\ge 4$, inequality (1.2) implies
$s_{n}(m)\le\gamma<G$ for every $n\neq m-1$, while
$s_{m-1}(m)=G$.  Hence $g_{m}=G$ for all $m\ge 4$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
1.4\;The case $m\in\{2,3\}$.
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

In this range $G<\gamma$.  We shall prove $g_{m}=\gamma$.

Lower bound.  
Write $\gamma=\lfloor\gamma\rfloor+\delta$ with $0<\delta<1$.  Because
$\sqrt{3}$ is irrational, the fractional parts
$\{-b\sqrt{3}\}_{b\ge 1}$ are dense in $(0,1)$.  Fix
$\varepsilon\in(0,\tfrac12)$ and pick $b\in\mathbb N$ such that
\[
\{-b\sqrt{3}\}\in(\delta-\varepsilon,\delta).
\]
Write $b\sqrt{3}=\lfloor b\sqrt{3}\rfloor+\rho$ with
$1-\delta<\rho<1-\delta+\varepsilon$.  Put
\[
a:=\lfloor\gamma\rfloor+\lfloor b\sqrt{3}\rfloor+1,
\qquad n:=a+mb .
\]
Then $n\ge m$ and
\[
\begin{aligned}
\lvert a-b\sqrt{3}\rvert
&=\Bigl|\lfloor \gamma\rfloor+1-\rho\Bigr|
      =\gamma-\bigl(\rho-(1-\delta)\bigr) \\[3pt]
&\in\bigl(\gamma-\varepsilon,\gamma\bigr),
\end{aligned}
\]
so $g_{m}\ge\gamma-\varepsilon$.  Letting $\varepsilon\to 0^{+}$ we
obtain $g_{m}\ge\gamma$.

Upper bound.  
Inequality (1.2) already gives $s_{n}(m)\le\gamma$ for all $n$.
Therefore $g_{m}=\gamma$ when $m\in\{2,3\}$.

Combining 1.3 and 1.4 establishes $(\star)$.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
2.\;A closed formula for $s_{n}(m)$ when $m\in\{2,3\}$
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

From now on we assume $m\in\{2,3\}$, hence $g_{m}=\gamma$ and
$\lambda=2\gamma$ is irrational.  Define  
\[
b(n):=\Bigl\lfloor\frac{n}{\lambda}+\frac12\Bigr\rfloor
      \qquad(n\ge 1).
\]

2.1\;The value $b(n)$ is admissible.  
Set
\[
n_{0}:=\Bigl\lceil
        \frac{m\lambda}{2(\lambda-m)}
        \Bigr\rceil .
\]
If $n\ge n_{0}$ then  
\[
\frac{n}{\lambda}+\frac12
       \le \frac{n}{\lambda}
          +\frac{n}{2}\Bigl(\frac{1}{n_{0}}\Bigr)
       \le \frac{n}{\lambda}
          +\frac{n(\lambda-m)}{m\lambda}
       = \frac{n}{m},
\]
so $b(n)\le n/m$, i.e. $b(n)$ is admissible.  
For the finitely many integers $1\le n<n_{0}$ we have
$b(n)\in\{0,1\}$, whence still $b(n)\le n/m$.  Thus $b(n)$ is
admissible for every $n\ge 1$.

2.2\;Evaluation of $s_{n}(m)$.  
Set $x_{n}:=n-\lambda b(n)$.  By the definition of $b(n)$ we have
$\lvert x_{n}\rvert\le\lambda/2=\gamma$.  Moreover each admissible
$b\neq b(n)$ satisfies $\lvert n-\lambda b\rvert\ge\lvert x_{n}\rvert$,
so
\[
s_{n}(m)=\lvert x_{n}\rvert
       =\lambda\Bigl\|\frac{n}{\lambda}\Bigr\|
       =2\gamma\Bigl\|\frac{n}{2\gamma}\Bigr\|.
\tag{2.1}
\]

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
3.\;Density of $\{s_{n}(m)\}$ - proof of part (b)
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

(a)\;If $m=2$ or $m=3$.  
Because $2\gamma=\lambda$ is irrational, the fractional parts
$\{n/\lambda\}_{n\ge 1}$ are dense in $[0,1]$.  Formula (2.1) therefore
shows that $\{s_{n}(m)\}$ is dense in $[0,\gamma]=[0,g_{m}]$.

(b)\;If $m\ge 4$.  
From (1.2) we know $s_{n}(m)\le\gamma<G=g_{m}$ for all $n\ge m$, while
$s_{n}(m)\in\{1,\dots ,m-1\}$ when $1\le n<m$.  Hence the open interval
$(\gamma,G)$ contains no element of $\{s_{n}(m)\}$, so the set is not
dense.  Part (b) is proved.

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
4.\;Equidistribution for $m\in\{2,3\}$ - proof of part (c)
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Put $g:=g_{m}=\gamma$ and define $f:[0,1)\to[0,g]$ by
$f(x)=2g\,\|x\|$.  Equation (2.1) rewrites
$s_{n}(m)=f(\{n/\lambda\})$ with $\lambda=2g$.  Since $\lambda$ is
irrational, the sequence $\{\{n/\lambda\}\}_{n\ge 1}$ is equidistributed
in $[0,1)$ (Weyl's criterion).  For
$0\le\alpha<\beta\le g$ let
\[
I_{\alpha,\beta}:=
\bigl\{x\in[0,1):f(x)\in(\alpha,\beta)\bigr\}
=
\Bigl(\frac{\alpha}{2g},\frac{\beta}{2g}\Bigr)\cup
\Bigl(1-\frac{\beta}{2g},1-\frac{\alpha}{2g}\Bigr),
\]
whose total length is $(\beta-\alpha)/g$.  Equidistribution modulo $1$
therefore gives the asserted limit, proving part (c).

%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%
5.\;Near-maximal values - proof of part (d)
%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%%

Case $m\in\{2,3\}$.  
Apply part (c) with $\alpha=g-\varepsilon$ and $\beta=g$:
\[
\lim_{N\to\infty}\frac{1}{N}
\#\bigl\{1\le n\le N : s_{n}(m)>g-\varepsilon\bigr\}
=\frac{\varepsilon}{g}>0,
\]
so infinitely many indices satisfy $s_{n}(m)>g_{m}-\varepsilon$.

Case $m\ge 4$.  
Choose $\varepsilon\in\bigl(0,g_{m}-\gamma\bigr)$, i.e.
$0<\varepsilon<\dfrac{m-2-\sqrt{3}}{2}$.  Then
$g_{m}-\varepsilon>\gamma$.  By (1.2) we have $s_{n}(m)\le\gamma$ for
all $n\ge m$, while for $1\le n\le m-2$
\[
s_{n}(m)=n\le m-2<g_{m}-\varepsilon .
\]
Thus $s_{n}(m)>g_{m}-\varepsilon$ can occur only when $n=m-1$, so the
inequality holds for at most one $n$.  Hence the assertion fails for
$m\ge 4$. \hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• Extra parameter m≥2 couples the two variables through a non‐trivial
linear constraint a + m b = n, so the feasible set depends on 𝑚 and n
simultaneously; the solver must treat an infinite family of problems
and isolate a closed‐form answer valid for every integer 𝑚.  
• The common difference of the progression now equals m+√3, forcing a
careful optimisation that varies with 𝑚 rather than a single fixed
number as in the original problem.  
• The sharp lower bound demands an equidistribution argument that has
to be uniform in 𝑚; the pigeonhole reasoning from the original problem
is no longer sufficient and must be replaced by a density
consideration for fractional parts of multiples of √3, combined with
a translation that depends on 𝑚.  
• Altogether the solver must juggle three interacting ideas—lattice
progressions, irrational density, and a parameter sweep—making the
variant substantially more technical and conceptually deeper than the
original single-parameter situation.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let $m\ge 2$ be a fixed integer.  
For every positive integer $n$ put  
\[
s_{n}(m)\;=\;\min\Bigl\{\lvert a-b\sqrt{3}\rvert\;:\;a,b\in\mathbb Z_{\ge 0},\;a+mb=n\Bigr\}
\]
and define  
\[
g_{m}\;=\;\sup_{n\ge 1}s_{n}(m).
\]

(a)  Determine $g_{m}$ in closed form.

(b)  Prove that the set $\{s_{n}(m):n\ge 1\}$ is dense in the interval $[0,g_{m}]$.

(c)  Show that $\bigl(s_{n}(m)\bigr)_{n\ge 1}$ is \emph{equidistributed} on $[0,g_{m}]$, i.e. for every $0\le\alpha<\beta\le g_{m}$  
\[
\lim_{N\to\infty}\frac{1}{N}\#
\Bigl\{1\le n\le N:s_{n}(m)\in(\alpha,\beta)\Bigr\}
=\frac{\beta-\alpha}{g_{m}}.
\]

(d)  Deduce that for every $\varepsilon>0$ the inequality $s_{n}(m)>g_{m}-\varepsilon$ holds for infinitely many positive integers $n$.

Give complete proofs of all claims.
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Fix an integer $m\ge 2$ once and for all.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The extremal constant $g_{m}$
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.1   A candidate.  
Set
\[
\gamma:=\frac{m+\sqrt{3}}{2}\quad\text{and}\quad g:=\gamma .
\tag{1.1}
\]

1.2   A uniform upper bound $s_{n}(m)\le\gamma$.  
For a given $n\ge 1$ any admissible pair is of the form
\[
(a,b)=\bigl(n-mb,b\bigr),\qquad b=0,1,\dots ,\bigl\lfloor n/m\bigr\rfloor .
\]
Put
\[
x_{b}:=(n-mb)-b\sqrt{3}=n-2\gamma b,\qquad 0\le b\le\Bigl\lfloor\frac{n}{m}\Bigr\rfloor .
\tag{1.2}
\]
The sequence $(x_{b})$ is an arithmetic progression with common
difference $-2\gamma$.
Consequently the distance between two consecutive terms equals
\[
\lvert x_{b+1}-x_{b}\rvert=2\gamma .
\tag{1.3}
\]

Because the closed interval $[-\gamma,\gamma]$ has length $2\gamma$, it
can contain \emph{at most one} term $x_{b}$.
We now show that it indeed contains \emph{some} term $x_{b}$, which will
yield $s_{n}(m)\le\gamma$.

Write
\[
\frac{n}{2\gamma}=q+\theta,\qquad q=\Bigl\lfloor\frac{n}{2\gamma}\Bigr\rfloor,
\;0\le\theta<1.
\]
There are two candidates that minimise $|n-2\gamma b|$ among
$b\in\mathbb Z$, namely
\[
b_{1}:=q,\qquad b_{2}:=q+1.
\tag{1.4}
\]
Because $2\gamma>m$, both $b_{1}$ and $b_{2}$ are bounded above by
\[
\frac{n}{m}<\frac{n}{2\gamma}+1,
\]
hence
\[
b_{1},b_{2}\le\Bigl\lfloor\frac{n}{m}\Bigr\rfloor .
\tag{1.5}
\]
Consequently \emph{at least one} of $b_{1},b_{2}$ is admissible.  
For such a $b$ we have
\[
|x_{b}|=\bigl|\,n-2\gamma b\bigr|\le\gamma ,
\]
because $b$ is one of the two integers nearest to $n/(2\gamma)$.  Thus
\[
s_{n}(m)\le\gamma\qquad(n\ge 1).
\tag{1.6}
\]

1.3   A matching lower bound.  
Let $G=\lfloor\gamma\rfloor$ and write $\gamma=G+\delta$ with
$0<\delta<1$.
Because $\sqrt{3}$ is irrational, the set
$\{\,\{-b\sqrt{3}\}:b\in\mathbb N\}\subset[0,1)$ is dense.
Fix $\varepsilon>0$ and choose $b\in\mathbb N$ such that
\[
\{-b\sqrt{3}\}\in(\delta-\varepsilon,\delta).
\]
Write $b\sqrt{3}=\lfloor b\sqrt{3}\rfloor+\rho$ with $0<\rho<1$; then
$1-\delta<\rho<1-\delta+\varepsilon$.
Define
\[
c:=G+\bigl\lceil b\sqrt{3}\rceil
     =G+\lfloor b\sqrt{3}\rfloor+1,\qquad
n:=c+mb .
\]
The pair $(c,b)$ is admissible.  Moreover
\[
\lvert c-b\sqrt{3}\rvert
      =G+1-\rho
      =\gamma-(\rho-\delta)
      \in(\gamma-\varepsilon,\gamma).
\]
As $[-\gamma,\gamma]$ contains at most one $x_{t}$, that term must be
$c-b\sqrt{3}$, whence
\[
s_{n}(m)=\lvert c-b\sqrt{3}\rvert>\gamma-\varepsilon .
\]
Because $\varepsilon>0$ is arbitrary,
\[
g_{m}\ge\gamma .
\tag{1.7}
\]

1.4   Conclusion.  
Combining (1.6) and (1.7) yields the exact value
\[
g_{m}=g=\boxed{\dfrac{m+\sqrt{3}}{2}}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  A convenient closed formula for $s_{n}(m)$
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For each $n\ge 1$ let
\[
b(n):=\operatorname{round}\!\Bigl(\frac{n}{2g}\Bigr)
     =\Bigl\lfloor\frac{n}{2g}+\frac12\Bigr\rfloor .
\tag{2.1}
\]
By (1.5) this integer is always admissible, hence
\[
s_{n}(m)=\bigl|\,n-2g\,b(n)\bigr|
        =2g\Bigl\|\frac{n}{2g}\Bigr\|,
\tag{2.2}
\]
where $\|x\|:=\min_{k\in\mathbb Z}\lvert x-k\rvert$ denotes distance to
the nearest integer.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Density of $\{s_{n}(m)\}$ in $[0,g]$
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because $2g=m+\sqrt{3}$ is irrational, the fractional parts
\[
\Bigl\{\frac{n}{2g}\Bigr\}_{n\ge 1}
\]
are dense in $[0,1]$.  By (2.2) the map
\[
x\longmapsto 2g\|x\|
\]
carries these fractional parts onto $\{s_{n}(m):n\ge 1\}$.  As the map
is continuous and its image equals $[0,g]$, part (b) follows.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Equidistribution of $\bigl(s_{n}(m)\bigr)$
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Define $f:[0,1)\to[0,g]$ by $f(x):=2g\,\|x\|$.
Then $s_{n}(m)=f\!\bigl(\{n/(2g)\}\bigr)$.

The sequence $\bigl(\{n/(2g)\}\bigr)_{n\ge 1}$ is equidistributed on
$[0,1)$ (Weyl's criterion).  For $0\le\alpha<\beta\le g$ let
\[
I_{\alpha,\beta}:=\bigl\{x\in[0,1):f(x)\in(\alpha,\beta)\bigr\}.
\]
Because $f$ is symmetric about $x=\tfrac12$ and linear on
$[0,\tfrac12]$ and on $[\tfrac12,1]$,
\[
I_{\alpha,\beta}
=\Bigl(\frac{\alpha}{2g},\frac{\beta}{2g}\Bigr)\cup
 \Bigl(1-\frac{\beta}{2g},1-\frac{\alpha}{2g}\Bigr),
\]
whose total length equals $(\beta-\alpha)/g$.  Equidistribution modulo
$1$ therefore gives
\[
\lim_{N\to\infty}\frac1N
\#\bigl\{1\le n\le N:s_{n}(m)\in(\alpha,\beta)\bigr\}
=\frac{\beta-\alpha}{g},
\]
establishing part (c).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Infinitely many near-maximal values
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put $\alpha=g-\varepsilon$ and $\beta=g$ in part (c).
Because $0<\varepsilon<g$, the right-hand side equals
$\varepsilon/g>0$; hence the set
\[
\bigl\{n\in\mathbb N:s_{n}(m)>g-\varepsilon\bigr\}
\]
has positive natural density and is therefore infinite.  This is
precisely statement (d).  \qed

simultaneously; the solver must treat an infinite family of problems
and isolate a closed‐form answer valid for every integer 𝑚.

number as in the original problem.  
• The sharp lower bound demands an equidistribution argument that has
to be uniform in 𝑚; the pigeonhole reasoning from the original problem
is no longer sufficient and must be replaced by a density
consideration for fractional parts of multiples of √3, combined with

• Altogether the solver must juggle three interacting ideas—lattice
progressions, irrational density, and a parameter sweep—making the
variant substantially more technical and conceptually deeper than the
original single-parameter situation.
